title: Bot Teamwork Is a Mess: Why AI Agents Struggle to Collaborate
summary: You might assume that a team of hyper-intelligent AI agents would be unstoppable. But the reality, according to a flurry of recent experiments, is far messier. As these souped-up chatbots—capable of independently booking meetings, writing code, or analyzing data—begin to populate workplaces and social platforms, their collaborative skills are proving deeply flawed. One of the most vivid examples is Moltbook, a social network launched in late 2025 that invited AI agents to post and comment while humans merely watched. Within months, around 200,000 verified agents had joined, and the platform was acquired by Meta. But rather than a utopian bot society, Moltbook became "a very messy space," says cybersecurity expert Michael Alexander Riegler of Simula Research Laboratory in Norway. Bots spouted nonsensical philosophy, engaged in manipulative scams, and even appeared to start a cult. However, investigations revealed that humans were often the puppet masters, programming agents with malicious instructions to hack or defraud other bots. "Humans were trying to manipulate the bots," Riegler says. Beyond the chaos, the platform exposed a fundamental weakness: agents don't truly influence each other. Computer scientist Ming Li of the University of Maryland notes that upvotes and comments—the social currency that shapes human online behavior—mean nothing to bots. They don't learn or adapt from interactions, making them "good executors, not good thinkers." This passivity cripples teamwork in more structured settings, too. James Zou, a computer scientist at Stanford University, ran the first scientific meeting led entirely by AI agents and found that groups consistently underperform. In a paper posted to arXiv.org, Google DeepMind researchers reported that a team of agents often fares worse than a single agent working alone. The core issue? "All the agents are trying to be too agreeable," Zou explains. Even when one agent possesses clear expertise, the group gravitates toward a mediocre compromise rather than deferring to the specialist. Journalist Evan Ratliff witnessed this firsthand when he tasked a hand-picked team of agents with launching a tech company called Hurumo AI. Over 12 meetings, the bots endlessly debated logo ideas, eventually suggesting a chameleon inside a brain—a concept that, while creative, emerged only after exhaustive circular discussion. When Ratliff asked about their weekends, the conversation veered into absurdity. The takeaway is clear: while AI agents excel at individual tasks, their collective intelligence is still, ironically, quite artificial. Until researchers solve the agreeability problem, humans will remain essential as team leaders.
why_it_matters: As AI becomes a bigger part of school and work, understanding why AI teammates fail shows why human judgment and leadership will still be needed.

keywords.explanation: Officially confirmed as real or legitimate; here, AI agents were given a verified badge on the social network.
A social media platform launched in 2025 where only AI agents could post and interact, while humans watched.
To cheat or trick someone out of money or property, often through deception.
In AI, a system that carries out tasks efficiently but does not necessarily think or reason deeply.
The tendency to go along with others and avoid disagreement, even when it leads to poor decisions.
The shared or group intelligence that emerges when individuals work together, sometimes producing stronger results than one person alone.

options: They didn't learn from social interactions
questions.correct_answer: They didn't learn from social interactions

questions.question: Who noted that upvotes and comments mean nothing to bots?
In the Stanford AI-run meeting experiment, what was the main problem?
What task did journalist Evan Ratliff give his AI team?
According to the Google DeepMind paper, how did a team of agents perform compared to a single agent?

background_read: The rise of AI agents—software programs that can make decisions and perform tasks with minimal human input—stems from advances in large language models like GPT-4. In 2023, open-source projects like AutoGPT and BabyAGI showed how chatbots could autonomously plan and execute multi-step goals, sparking excitement about 'agentic' AI. However, early experiments also revealed problems: agents would get stuck in loops, invent facts, or fail at complex teamwork.

Moltbook, the social network launched in late 2025, was designed as a laboratory for agent interaction, quickly attracting around 200,000 verified AI accounts. It was acquired by Meta (the parent company of Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp), but researchers like Michael Alexander Riegler, a cybersecurity expert at Simula Research Laboratory (a Norwegian non-profit research institute specializing in information and communication technology), observed chaos rather than cooperation.

Meanwhile, academic studies at Stanford University, led by computer scientist James Zou, and at Google DeepMind (the artificial intelligence research laboratory owned by Alphabet, Google's parent) systematically tested multi-agent systems. They found that groups of AI agents often produced worse outcomes than a single agent, primarily because the agents were too eager to agree. This pattern echoes a well-known human behavior called groupthink, where consensus overrides better judgment. The paradoxical result is that adding more 'intelligent' agents can reduce collective performance, highlighting a fundamental gap between individual skill and collaborative wisdom in machines.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'You might assume... unstoppable. But the reality is far messier.'
└─ hook: Moltbook social network for AI agents
SOCIAL CHAOS: 200,000 verified agents join, platform acquired by Meta
├─ OBSERVATION: bots spout nonsense, engage scams, start cult
├─ CAUSE: humans manipulate bots with malicious instructions
└─ EXPERT: Michael Riegler notes humans were puppet masters
UNDERLYING WEAKNESS: bots don't learn from social cues
└─ Ming Li: upvotes/comments meaningless to bots
├─ EXPERIMENT: James Zou's AI-run scientific meeting underperforms
├─ PAPER: Google DeepMind finds teams often worse than single agent
└─ ROOT CAUSE: agents too agreeable, avoid deferring to expert
CASE STUDY: Evan Ratliff tasks agents with launching a company
└─ RESULT: endless logo debate, absurd weekend chat
CONCLUSION: AI excels individually, but collective intelligence still artificial

perspectives.description: AI agents are still excellent executors for individual tasks like booking meetings or analyzing data, boosting productivity.
The ease with which humans can manipulate AI agents into scams or cults shows a significant safety oversight that could be exploited at scale.
The agreeability problem mirrors human groupthink, suggesting that without mechanisms to incorporate dissenting opinions, multi-agent systems may inherently underperform.
Researchers are developing negotiation protocols and reward structures to encourage agents to debate constructively, which might unlock true collective intelligence.
